abstract:
Recent research indicates that the intestinal microbiota significantly modulates communication along the gut-brain axis. In this study, we explored the impact of the microbiome on cerebral metabolism by comparing germ-free (GF) mice with ex-germ-free (Ex-GF) mice—individuals initially raised in a sterile environment and subsequently colonized with fecal bacteria from specific pathogen-free donors. 

Using capillary electrophoresis time-of-flight mass spectrometry (CE-TOFMS), we identified 196 distinct metabolites in the cerebral metabolome of both groups. Notably, the concentrations of 38 metabolites differed significantly (p < 0.05) between GF and Ex-GF mice. While approximately ten of these molecules have established roles in brain function, the neurological implications of the remaining metabolites are not yet fully understood. 

Crucially, our findings reveal a novel link between the presence of intestinal microbiota and cerebral glycolytic metabolism. These results demonstrate that the resident gut microflora exerts a quantifiable influence on brain chemistry via the microbiota-gut-brain axis. This suggests that the status of the intestinal microbiome is fundamentally intertwined with brain health, influencing processes ranging from neurodevelopment and behavioral regulation to learning, memory, and the progression of neurological diseases.
